title: Computer Networks by Scale: Vocabulary & Questions

exercises.sourceTaskName: Vocabulary and Grammar Work, Task 1: Science and technology jobs

questions.grammarRule: 'Science' is the general subject a scientist studies.
'Medicine' is the subject a doctor studies.
'Psychology' is the subject a psychologist studies (the initial 'p' is silent when pronounced).
'Engineering' is the subject an engineer studies.
'Architecture' is the subject an architect studies.
'Geology' is the subject a geologist studies.
'Do an experiment' is the standard collocation; 'make an experiment' isn't natural English.
'Carry out research' is the standard collocation; 'make research' isn't natural English.
'Investigate' takes a direct object ('investigates problems'); 'look' would need 'look INTO problems' to work the same way.
'Draw (up) plans' is the standard collocation for creating building plans; 'write plans' isn't natural here.
'Carry out tests' is the standard collocation; 'execute' is more typically used for carrying out programs/commands, not physical tests.
'Make a calculation' is a standard collocation; 'find a calculation' isn't natural English.
'Treat patients' takes a direct object; 'care' would need 'care FOR patients' to work the same way.
'Give advice' is the standard collocation; you 'tell someone something', but you 'give advice'.
'Take samples' is the standard collocation; 'look' would need 'look AT samples' to work the same way.

promptParts.before: A geologist takes rock samples and

questions.grammarRule: 'Analyse' fits examining samples scientifically; 'prove' doesn't fit this context.

exercises.sourceTaskName: Vocabulary and Grammar Work, Task 2: Adjectives to describe jobs

promptParts.before: Which adjective means "making you feel that you want to sleep or rest"?

questions.grammarRule: 'Tiring' describes something that makes you want to sleep or rest, often from physical or mental exhaustion.

promptParts.before: Which adjective means "makes you worry a lot"?

questions.grammarRule: 'Stressful' describes something that makes you worry or feel under pressure a lot.

promptParts.before: Which adjective means "difficult in an interesting or enjoyable way"?

questions.grammarRule: 'Challenging' describes something difficult in a way that's stimulating rather than just hard.

promptParts.before: Which adjective means "involving different kinds of things or people"?

questions.grammarRule: 'Varied' describes something made up of many different kinds of things or people.

promptParts.before: Which adjective means "producing or using new or imaginative ideas"?

questions.grammarRule: 'Creative' describes producing or using new, imaginative ideas.

promptParts.before: Which adjective means "making you feel very sad"?

questions.grammarRule: 'Depressing' describes something that makes you feel very sad or low.

promptParts.before: Which adjective means "making you feel happy and satisfied because you're doing something useful or important"?

questions.grammarRule: 'Rewarding' describes something that makes you feel happy and satisfied because you feel you're doing something useful or important.

promptParts.before: Steve is asking Phil about his girlfriend Cinzia. Prompt: "Which part of Italy / she / come from?" Question:

options: Which part of Italy does she come from?
Which part of Italy she comes from?
Which part of Italy is she coming from?
Which part of Italy she does come from?
correctAnswers: Which part of Italy does she come from?
questions.grammarRule: Wh-questions about habitual facts need do-support and standard question word order: 'does she come from', not 'she comes from' or 'is she coming from' (which wrongly implies right now).
A completed past event needs simple past with do-support and standard word order: 'did you meet', not 'you met' (missing auxiliary) or 'do you meet' (wrong tense).

promptParts.before: Prompt: "What / she / do for a living?" Question:

options: What does she do for a living?
What she does for a living?
What is she doing for a living?
What she do for a living?
correctAnswers: What does she do for a living?
questions.grammarRule: Asking about a permanent occupation needs present simple with do-support: 'does she do', not 'is she doing' (which wrongly implies right now) or 'she does' (wrong word order).

promptParts.before: Prompt: "When / she first / come / to England?" Question:

options: When did she first come to England?
When she first came to England?
When does she first come to England?
When has she first come to England?
correctAnswers: When did she first come to England?
questions.grammarRule: A specific past event needs simple past with do-support: 'did she first come', not 'she first came' (missing auxiliary/wrong word order).

promptParts.before: Prompt: "How well / she / speak English?" Question:

options: How well does she speak English?
How well is she speaking English?
How well she does speak English?
correctAnswers: How well does she speak English?
questions.grammarRule: Asking about a general ability needs present simple with do-support: 'does she speak', not 'is she speaking' (which wrongly implies right now) or 'she speaks' (wrong word order).
'Like' is a stative verb (not used in continuous form), and yes/no questions need do-support at the front: 'Does she like...?', not 'Is she liking...?' or 'She likes...?'.

promptParts.before: Prompt: "she / have / a flat here?" Question:

options: Does she have a flat here?
Is she having a flat here?
She does have a flat here?
correctAnswers: Does she have a flat here?
questions.grammarRule: 'Have' (meaning 'possess') is a stative verb, and yes/no questions need do-support at the front: 'Does she have...?', not 'Is she having...?' or 'She has...?'.

promptParts.before: An official is asking Sarah questions about emigrating. Prompt: "Where / your husband / born?" Question:

options: Where has your husband been born?
questions.grammarRule: Being born is a completed past event described with the passive 'be born': 'was your husband born', not 'did your husband born' (which wrongly adds 'did' to an already-past-participle verb).
'Have' (meaning 'possess') is a stative verb, and yes/no questions need do-support at the front: 'Does he have...?', not 'Is he having...?' or 'He has...?'.

promptParts.before: Prompt: "How many children / you / have?" Question:

options: How many children do you have?
How many children are you having?
correctAnswers: How many children do you have?
questions.grammarRule: 'Have' (meaning 'possess') needs do-support in modern questions: 'do you have', not 'are you having' (stative verb) or 'have you' (an older, now non-standard inversion for this meaning).
'Old' is an adjective following the verb 'be' (not 'do' or 'have'), and 'children' is plural, so the verb must be 'are': 'How old are your children?'.
